title: Listening - Part 4: Designing Escape Rooms
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a man called Ben, who designs escape rooms. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Joining me today is Ben Holloway, who designs escape rooms, where teams solve puzzles in order to complete a story. Ben, how did you enter such an unusual business? || Ben: I was working in a hotel and arranging team-building activities for guests. One rainy weekend, all the outdoor plans were cancelled, so I made a simple mystery game inside the hotel. [24]The guests enjoyed my improvised indoor puzzle activity so much that I began thinking it could become a full-time job[/24]. I didn’t even know the term ‘escape room’ then. || Presenter: Where do your ideas for rooms come from? || Ben: Films can provide an atmosphere, but copying a film plot rarely works because players need to take action. I start by imagining a situation: perhaps a scientist has disappeared or a train is about to leave. [25]Only after I know what the players are trying to achieve do I invent the puzzles[/25]. That way the activities belong to the story rather than feeling random. || Presenter: Is making puzzles difficult? || Ben: Very. A designer knows the solution already, so it is easy to assume a clue is obvious. During testing, I watch groups without helping them. [26]The greatest challenge is creating puzzles that make players think hard without leaving them stuck and annoyed[/26]. A puzzle should produce a moment of satisfaction, not make people feel foolish. || Presenter: Do teams ever surprise you? || Ben: Regularly. In one room, players had to find a number hidden in a painting. I had prepared several clues leading them towards it. Then [27]one group noticed the answer immediately because a lamp reflected on the painting in a way I hadn’t planned[/27]. We moved the lamp after that; an accidental shortcut can ruin the experience. || Presenter: What has changed in your rooms over time? || Ben: Our first games contained several padlocks and printed notes, because they were cheap and easy to reset. People still enjoy those, but expectations are higher now. [28]We now use sound, lighting and moving objects to make players feel as if their decisions are changing the room around them[/28]. The technology supports the story; it should never replace good puzzles. || Presenter: Some people dislike the idea of being locked in. || Ben: The door is never truly locked, and we explain that clearly. Players can leave at any moment. In fact, [29]making people feel safe before the game begins helps them concentrate on enjoying the challenge[/29]. Anxiety is no fun, and no successful game depends on it. || Presenter: What makes a good escape-room player? || Ben: People think it is the cleverest person in the team. I disagree. [30]Successful teams share every discovery aloud instead of allowing one person to solve everything alone[/30]. A tiny observation in somebody’s hand may connect with information another player has found.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: Why did Ben first consider designing escape rooms professionally?

options.A: Hotel guests responded well to a game he made.
options.B: His employer asked him to design indoor rooms.
options.C: A film producer suggested an idea to him.

questions.explanation: His improvised puzzle game for hotel guests was very successful.

questions.question: What does Ben decide before designing the puzzles?

options.A: The amount of technology the room will need.
options.B: The objective players will have in the story.
options.C: The films on which the room will be based.

questions.explanation: He first establishes what players are trying to achieve, then makes the puzzles.

questions.question: What does Ben find most difficult about creating puzzles?

options.A: Avoiding puzzles that are either too easy or too frustrating.
options.B: Finding enough people to test each game thoroughly.
options.C: Ensuring players cannot damage the objects in a room.

questions.explanation: He aims for puzzles that require thought but do not leave players stuck and annoyed.

questions.question: How did a group of players surprise Ben?

options.A: They solved a puzzle by using an unintended clue.
options.B: They refused to use the clues he offered them.
options.C: They found an object that was missing from the room.

questions.explanation: A lamp reflection accidentally revealed an answer much earlier than planned.

questions.question: How have Ben's escape rooms developed?

options.A: They now include fewer stories and more difficult puzzles.
options.B: They use effects that respond to what players do.
options.C: They are cheaper to prepare between games.

questions.explanation: Sound, lighting and moving objects now make players feel their actions affect the room.

questions.question: Why does Ben explain that players can leave a room whenever they want?

options.A: It stops players asking for unnecessary clues.
options.B: It reduces the time needed to run the game.
options.C: It allows players to feel comfortable during the activity.

questions.explanation: Feeling safe helps players enjoy and focus on the challenge.

questions.question: According to Ben, what do the best teams do?

options.A: They allow the most intelligent member to lead.
options.B: They tell each other what they have found.
options.C: They solve each puzzle before searching further.

questions.explanation: Ben says successful teams communicate every discovery.
